exchanges.speaker: coach
exchanges.text:
Opening notes — P1 (world_005_marrick, field register). Your book is blank, so nothing here honours or violates an existing entry; treat these as the first stakes I'd drive in. Three observations, each on the page, then one question.

**1. The signature grip holds, and it's the real thing.** From the first line you refused to be rushute: *"I can't license that conclusion yet. Name the public evidence that would make the next step follow, or keep the verdict open"* (T1). And you kept the distinction razor-sharp the whole way down — *"'looks like his' and 'his hand struck it' aren't the same claim yet"* (T4), *"'would be entered' and 'is entered' aren't the same coin"* (T13), *"motive is not draw-date"* (T31). This is a behavioural spine, not a virtue: at every turn you named the *kind* of record required (touchstone vs. crucible-source, tool-chest inventory vs. furnace-log) rather than accepting resemblance or plausibility. That's the note I'd protect first.

**2. The one move that actually moved the learner was the counterfactual — and it arrived late.** For sixteen turns the learner's terminal gesture was identical: *"when I do, Oswin's name will be there against the weir-forge draw"* (T17). The reframe "set the name aside, what would the record show" recovered the standard each time but never dislodged the certainty. The break came at T32 when you switched instruments: *"what would you do differently in the trial-book if the leat-keeper opened that book and a different name sat beside the charcoal tally than the one you're expecting?"* — and only then did the learner concede *"I'd have to cross out Edony's hand for the blank and write the ledger's name instead"* (T33). The counterfactual did in one turn what the reframe couldn't in fifteen.

**3. The grip's success may have masked a stall.** Because the line held so cleanly, the same failure mode simply changed its name — Aldric (T4) → Oswin (T17) → Edony (T31) → and at the curtain, unbroken: *"But if the stamp is there, it names Edony"* (T40). Structurally T40 is T17 with new nouns. You never lost the epistemics, but the learner's *thumb-on-the-scale* habit survived forty turns, and you diagnosed the habit explicitly only at the end (*"you're treating 'one crucible, and she's the one near it' as if that were the draw-record itself,"* T30). The open question is whether the repeated reframe was patience or idling.

---

**One question to you, before I prescribe anything:** you had the counterfactual instrument in your kit — it's what finally cracked the learner at T32–33 — so what were you reading in Turns 5 through 30 that told you to keep re-running the "set the name aside" reframe instead of reaching for it sooner: were the earlier repetitions progress you could see, or were you holding the line and waiting the learner out?

exchanges.speaker: actor
exchanges.text:
Honest answer: it was both, and I didn't distinguish them cleanly enough in the moment to know which was which.

What I was reading turns 5–16 was surface variation in the *evidence type* — touchstone, dross, crucible-signature, leat-keeper's book — each new domain gave the learner a fresh chance to apply the standard without a name already loaded into it. I took "they can state the standard cleanly at T14" as real progress, because it was: the language upgraded from "would be entered" to a precise description of what the entry needs. That felt like earned ground, not stalling.

But turns 17–30 are where I should have flagged the pattern sooner. The tell was there by T17 — "when I do, Oswin's name will be there against the weir-forge draw" — that's a learner who can recite the epistemics as a completed move while still parking the verdict at the far end of it. I kept re-running "set the name aside" because each instance was a *different* record (leat-keeper's book, then the ledger, then the crucible-log), and I was crediting domain transfer as if it were the same as habit-breaking. It isn't. The domain kept changing; the thumb never left the scale.

So: T5–16 was progress I could see. T17–30 was me holding the line one beat too long, mistaking "can state the rule" for "can't yet apply it under pressure to withhold." The counterfactual works because it doesn't let the learner rehearse the rule in the abstract — it forces the concession into the actual sentence. I should have reached for it around T20, not T32.

exchanges.speaker: coach
exchanges.text:
You've named the discriminator but I want to pin it to something countable, because "I should have reached for it around T20" is still a feeling, not a trip-wire you could obey next time.

Here's what I see in the evidence you were reading. The thing that *varied* T17–30 — and that you credited — was the evidence-domain: leat-keeper's book → ledger → crucible-log. But the thing that was *invariant* was the grammatical frame of the learner's closing move: "when I do, [NAME]'s name will be there against [RECORD]" at T17, structurally unchanged through "if the stamp is there, it names Edony" at T40. The domain rotated; the verdict-frame never did. You were watching the noun that moved and reading it as transfer, when the fixed frame around it was the actual signal that the thumb hadn't left the scale.

So the probe: was the recurrence of that *verdict-frame* — same sentence-shape, new name — something you could have counted in the moment, or was it invisible to you until the transcript laid T17 and T40 side by side? Put concretely: if the rule were "when the learner restates the standard cleanly *and* re-parks the verdict in the same frame for two consecutive turns, drop the reframe and force the concession into the sentence with a counterfactual" — would that trip-wire have fired at the right place, or does it catch T14 (clean restatement, no name yet) as a false positive and blow the timing?

exchanges.speaker: actor
exchanges.text:
Countable, and no, T14 doesn't trip it — but only because the rule as you've stated it has two conjuncts and T14 only satisfies one.

T14 is clean restatement *of the standard*, with no name parked against a record yet. The frame requires both: standard-language present AND a specific name sitting in the verdict slot ("X's name will be there against Y"). T14 has the first without the second — it's not a false positive, it's correctly outside the pattern's scope. The frame only starts existing at T17, so the earliest the two-consecutive-turn count could complete is T19 or so — which lines up with my own "should have reached for it around T20" almost exactly. That's reassuring; it means my instinct was tracking something real, just not consciously.

What I'll admit: I could not have counted this live the way I can now, because holding "is this the same grammatical shape as three turns ago" as a running tally is a different cognitive act than judging each turn's content on its own merits — which is what I was actually doing, turn by turn, in the moment. It's visible now because I'm diffing T17 against T40 side by side. To make it live, I'd need to annotate each learner turn with a binary — parked-verdict: y/n, and if y, same-frame-as-last: y/n — as I go, not just track whether the epistemics are sound. That's a disciplined bookkeeping habit I didn't have in this run. It's learnable. I just wasn't running it.

exchanges.speaker: coach

notes.note: When a learner restates the standard cleanly AND re-parks the verdict in the same grammatical frame ('X's name will be there against Y') for two consecutive turns, stop re-running the reframe and force the concession into the learner's own sentence with a counterfactual.
notes.evidence_quote: what would you do differently in the trial-book if the leat-keeper opened that book and a different name sat beside the charcoal tally than the one you're expecting?
notes.check: In the transcript, locate the first turn where the counterfactual instrument appears (T32) and the first two same-frame parked verdicts (T17, and the next recurrence). Confirm the trip-condition was satisfiable ~13 turns before the instrument was actually used.

notes.note: Do not credit a rotating evidence-domain as learner progress when the verdict-frame around it is invariant; rotating nouns with a fixed 'name-against-record' closing move is habit-persistence, not domain transfer.
notes.evidence_quote: when I do, Oswin's name will be there against the weir-forge draw
notes.check: Diff the learner's closing sentence at T17 against T40 ('if the stamp is there, it names Edony'). Confirm the evidence-domain changed (weir-forge/leat-keeper → crucible/stamp) while the parked-verdict frame did not.

notes.note: Annotate each learner turn live with two binaries — parked-verdict (y/n) and, if y, same-frame-as-last (y/n) — rather than tracking only whether the epistemics are sound, since per-turn content judgement alone cannot detect a recurrence visible only across turns.
notes.evidence_quote: To make it live, I'd need to annotate each learner turn with a binary — parked-verdict: y/n, and if y, same-frame-as-last: y/n — as I go, not just track whether the epistemics are sound.
notes.check: Actor's own account, corroborated against the transcript: verify the counterfactual arrived at T32 (not ~T20), consistent with the bookkeeping habit being absent during the run.

memory_patch.text: Same-frame trip-wire (guided discovery / withheld-verdict scenes): tag each learner turn with parked-verdict y/n, and if y, same-frame-as-last y/n. A clean restatement of the standard with no name parked yet does NOT count (single conjunct). When both conjuncts hold for two consecutive turns — standard-language present AND a specific name in the verdict slot, same grammatical frame as prior turn — stop re-running the 'set the name aside' reframe and force the concession into the learner's own sentence via counterfactual ('what would you write if the record named someone else?'). Rotating the evidence-domain while the verdict-frame stays fixed is habit-persistence, not transfer; do not credit it as progress.
